description: This figure plots Charpy V-notch (CVN) impact energy (unit: J) as a function of test temperature (range: -200°C to 100°C, unit: °C) for different Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels, with background shaded regions representing the impact energy ranges of austenitic, duplex, ferritic, and martensitic stainless steels for comparison. There are 5 tested curves: Curve 1 corresponds to Fe-28Mn-5Al-1C steel solutionized at 1050°C for 75 min (grain size 41 μm); Curve 2 is the same alloy solutionized at 1050°C for 8 h (grain size 135 μm); Curve 3 is Fe-30Mn-10Al-1C-1Si steel solutionized at 1050°C for 1 h (grain size 120 μm). These three solution-treated samples show high room-temperature impact energy (~200 J, comparable to austenitic stainless steels) and ductile-to-brittle transition temperature below -100°C. Curve 4 is the Curve 2 alloy after aging at 550°C for 16 h, with significantly reduced impact energy (~55 J at room temperature, decreasing continuously with falling temperature); Curve 5 is the Curve 3 alloy after the same aging treatment, with near-zero impact energy across all tested temperatures. The core conclusion is that as-solutionized austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steels possess excellent impact toughness matching austenitic stainless steels, while aging-induced κ-carbide precipitation leads to dramatic toughness deterioration, especially for Si-containing high-Al alloys which become nearly fully brittle after peak aging.
